texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Stochastic reaction networks are mathematical models frequently used in, but not limited to, biochemistry. These models are continuous-time Markov chains whose transition rates depend on certain parameters called rate constants , which despite the name may not be constant in real-world applications. In this paper we study how random switching between different stochastic reaction networks with asymptotically linear rate functions affects the stability of the process. We give matrix conditions for both positive recurrence (indeed, exponentially ergodicity) and transience (indeed, evanescence) in both the regime with high switching rates and the regime with low switching rates. We then make use of these conditions to provide examples of processes whose stability behavior changes as the switching rate varies. We also explore what happens in the middle regime where the switching rates are neither high nor low and our theorems do not apply. Specifically, we show by examples that there can be arbitrarily many phase transitions between exponentially ergodicity and evanescence as the switching rate increases.

texts.orig: 1 Introduction
texts.text: 1 Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A Chemical Reaction Network (CRN) is a set of possible chemical species, along with a finite set of chemical transformations that the species can undergo. These models are frequently used in biochemistry both for investigating existing systems and for designing new synthetic biological circuits. Their use also extends to epidemiology, ecology, genetics. It is impossible to cite all the applications present in the scientific literature; we only suggest a few works in the aforementioned directions and some comprehensive books: [4,9,12,13,16,17,23,32]. We also note that dynamical models of CRNs can be used as an alternative expression for families of polynomial differential equations or for stochastic models such as instances of continuous-time branching processes and Markovian queuing systems.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Deterministic and stochastic mass-action models of CRNs have been studied for over 50 years [18, 26]. These models are not fully determined by the set of possible reactions, but require an additional 'rate constant' for each reaction, whose chemical interpretation is the propensity of a collection of the reactants to actually undergo the reaction. These rate constants are not a property solely of the reactions themselves, as they are also affected by factors such as temperature [11, Section 1.6], and the availability of input species which are not modeled and assumed to be in abundance [15].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In regimes where the temperature or other relevant factors vary, it may be tempting to assume that the qualitative behavior of the system is well approximated by ignoring the variability of the rate constants, either by taking an average or by separately studying the system in the different regimes. However, recent work suggests that this assumption is dangerous. For example, consider piecewise-deterministic Markov processes (PDMPs) that take the form of models switching between two linear 2-dimensional ODEs. In [6] they demonstrated that such PDMPs could potentially be stable or unstable, depending only on the transition rates between the two ODEs. Notably, Example 1.3 in [6] consists of a PDMP that switches between two linear ODEs, each of which can be realized as a deterministic mass-action CRN. Thus, though this was not the point of the paper, it demonstrated that deterministic mass-action CRNs whose rate constants vary stochastically can be either stable or unstable depending only on the rate at which the rate constants vary, and thus the variability of the rate constants cannot be neglected. Similar conclusions could be drawn for deterministically switching models from the previous paper [5], that [6] builds upon. A survey of results on models with deterministic switching between linear ODEs can be found in [28]. PDMPs which switch between nonlinear ODEs have also been studied [7], as have diffusions in random environments [31].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the present paper, we investigate the stability of a stochastic process that randomly switches between a finite number of stochastic CRNs. We denote a choice of stochastic CRN as an 'environment,' and we assume the choice changes in time according to a finite continuous-time Markov chain. We further assume that the transition rates of the environment Markov chain are multiplied by a common factor κ > 0 (the bigger κ is, the faster the environment switches occur). See Figure 1 for an example of this. Our four main theorems give matrix conditions for stability and instability for κ large (Theorems 4 and 10, respectively) and similarly for κ small (Theorems 8 and 16, respectively). In a sense, our results address the question above of when is it safe to approximate the stability of the system by ignoring the variability of the rate constants. We prove that when the rate constants are changing rapidly enough, the system with averaged rate constants is qualitatively similar, whereas when the rate constants are changing slowly enough (subject to some exceptions; see Example 32), the correct approximation is by the constant rate processes rather than the average.

We further assume that the transition rates of the environment Markov chain are multiplied by a common factor κ > 0 (the bigger κ is, the faster the environment switches occur). See Figure 1 for an example of this. texts.label: text
texts.orig: The assumptions in our results match the conditions given in [6] in the PDMP case, although that paper only addresses two of the four possibilities (namely, stability for small κ and instability for large κ ; see their Theorem 1.6). Consistently, in their examples they show that (to use our terminology) the system can be stable for small κ but unstable for large κ , whereas we have examples both of this and of the reverse behavior (instability for small κ paired with stability for large κ ). There is a further difference with [6] that is worth emphasizing, in addition to their notable restriction to dimension 2: their proof is based on a polar decomposition technique, which allows them to reduce the dimension of the problem by exploiting the fact that the dynamics of the angle is autonomous. This technique does not apply in the context of this paper. Instead, we prove our results from scratch using a novel Lyapunov function construction, which has the added benefit of working in higher dimensions. In fact, our technique could potentially be adapted back to their setting, though we do not address that possibility in this work. A Lyapunov function approach is also used in [31], however, the Lyapunov functions we construct in this paper rely on minor while indispensable changes caused by the switching environments, while their Lyapunov function constructions are based on a state space decomposition.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Above, we have discussed our results in the cases where κ is sufficiently large or sufficiently small. We also study the case where κ takes some intermediate value. It turns out that in that case, regardless of the behavior at the extremes, the process can undergo an arbitrarily large number of phase transitions between stable and unstable. While similar counterintuitive behavior has been noticed in [27] in the context of PDMPs switching between linear ODEs, the models in that paper do not arise from CRNs. Moreover, though both here and in [27] the key first step for showing arbitrarily many phase transitions is to construct a model which is stable for large and small κ but is unstable for some intermediate κ , both the construction and verification are fundamentally different.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We would be remiss not to mention that we are not the first to look at specifically stochastic mass-action CRNs with stochastically varying coefficients. In [10], they give conditions for such networks to be positive recurrent regardless of κ . The monomolecular networks from that paper are a subclass of the networks studied in this one, though unlike the present paper they allow for the stochastic environment to have an infinite state space. The major difference in the two papers is that the conditions for positive recurrence in [10] are structural, in contrast with the matrix conditions given here; this means that the results in [10] cannot capture stability transitions of the type studied in this paper.

texts.orig: Finally, it is worth highlighting that the models we study, under the mass-action and at-most-monomolecular assumptions, are 'continuous-time multitype branching processes with immigration in a random environment.' While we are not aware of any results pertaining the stability of such general models, some related work exists for both discrete-time [22,34] and continuous-time [21] processes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the remainder of this section, we will describe our notational conventions and definitions, as well as the precise setting in which we work. In Sections 2 and 3, we provide sufficient conditions, in this setting, for models to be either exponentially ergodic or evanescent when κ is either sufficiently large or sufficiently small. Section 4 provides various examples of applying these theorems, including Examples 21 and 25 in which a stochastic mass-action CRN with stochastically varying rate constants transitions from stable to unstable or vice versa as κ increases. Section 5 deals with the case where κ in the intermediate region where none of our previous theorems apply; we do not give a complete classification but show via several examples that the spectrum of possible behaviors is very rich. Section 6 addresses a possible generalization of Theorem 16 which is left open by the present work. Lastly, the (already known) Lyapunov function theorems which undergird our proofs are provided in the Appendix, along with a summary of various results pertaining Metzler matrices which are useful for placing in context the hypotheses of our theorems.

texts.orig: The objects of study in this paper are chemical reactions networks , or CRNs . Let S be any finite set; we refer to the elements of S as species . Linear combination of species with coefficients in Z ≥ 0 are called complexes . Suppose we have a finite set R of reactions , each of which is an arrow from one complex (called the source complex ) to a second complex (called the product complex ). A CRN is a tuple ( S , C , R ) where S and R are as discussed and C is the set of all complexes which appear in at least one reaction (either as a source complex or a product complex). CRNs are frequently drawn as a directed graph whose vertices are the complexes and whose edges are the reactions. While strictly speaking complexes are linear combinations over Z ≥ 0 of species, it is standard to view them as elements of Z S ≥ 0 by identifying linear combinations with their coefficients, and is even more common to abuse notation further by implicitly fixing an ordering of the species and viewing complexes as elements of Z d ≥ 0 for d ∈ Z > 0 the number of species.
texts.text: The objects of study in this paper are chemical reactions networks , or CRNs . Let S be any finite set; we refer to the elements of S as species . Linear combination of species with coefficients in Z ≥ 0 are called complexes . Suppose we have a finite set R of reactions , each of which is an arrow from one complex (called the source complex ) to a second complex (called the product complex ). A CRN is a tuple ( S , C , R ) where S and R are as discussed and C is the set of all complexes which appear in at least one reaction (either as a source complex or a product complex). CRNs are frequently drawn as a directed graph whose vertices are the complexes and whose edges are the reactions. While strictly speaking complexes are linear combinations over Z ≥ 0 of species, it is standard to view them as elements of Z S ≥ 0 by identifying linear combinations with their coefficients, and is even more common to abuse notation further by implicitly fixing an ordering of the species and viewing complexes as elements of Z d ≥ 0 for d ∈ Z > 0 the number of species.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: One example CRN is ( S 1 , C 1 , R 1 ) with species S 1 = { H 2 O,H 2 , O 2 } , reaction R 1 = { 2 H 2 + O 2 → 2 H 2 O } , and complexes C 1 = { 2 H 2 + O 2 , 2 H 2 O } . Here the only source complex is 2 H 2 + O 2 and the only product complex is 2 H 2 O . We will say a CRN has at-most-monomolecular reactions if for each i and each y → y ′ ∈ R , the vector y ∈ Z d ≥ 0 has at most one entry which is one and all other entries are zero. The example ( S 1 , C 1 , R 1 ) from the beginning of this paragraph does not have at-most-monomolecular reactions because of the existence of a source complex 2 H 2 + O 2 , identified with the vector (2 , 1 , 0); an example of an at-most-monomolecular reaction network would be one consisting only of the two reactions 0 → S 1 → 2 S 2 , where 0 denotes the empty linear combination 0 S 1 +0 S 2 . Reactions with source complex 0 are referred to as inflow reactions .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We are particularly interested here in stochastic mass-action kinetics : to each reaction y → y ′ ∈ R , assign a number, or rate constant , κ y → y ′ ∈ R > 0 , and define the rate functions as λ y → y ′ ( x ) := κ y → y ′ ∏ d m =1 ∏ y m -1 j =0 ( x m -j ). Put another way, if the state space x ∈ Z d ≥ 0 is interpreted as a vector listing the number of each species, then the rate of reaction y → y ′ is directly proportional to the number of ways to select the input species y from the available species x , with constant of proportionality κ y → y ′ .

texts.orig: The intended interpretation of the SAL Setting is that there are n CRNs with a combined d species, and which one of the CRNs is active at any given time is decided by a further Markov chain (the stochastic environment) on [ n ] that evolves freely, with transition rates completely independent from the current species count. Then w i is the fraction of time which the CRN corresponding to M i is active, and so M is the matrix giving the average rate of change of each species as a function of each other species.

texts.text: Theorem 10: In the SAL Setting, suppose additionally that each CRN is endowed with mass-action kinetics and has at-most-monomolecular reactions. Suppose that there exists a vector v ∈ R d ≥ 0 such that ( vM ) m > 0 for each m ∈ supp( v ), where ( vM ) m is the m -th entry of the vector vM . Then as long as κ is sufficiently large, we have that ( x, i ) is an evanescent state for the mixed Markov chain Z κ for every i ∈ [ n ] and every x ∈ Z d ≥ 0 such that supp( v ) ∩ supp( x ) = ∅ .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Remark 11: The condition supp( v ) ∩ supp( x ) = ∅ means that x is non-zero in at least one coordinate in which v is non-zero. As for the transience or recurrence of the states where x is zero in every coordinate in which v is non-zero, there are two cases. The first is that, given such an x , some state z with supp( v ) ∩ supp( z ) = ∅ is reachable from x . In that case z is transient and hence so is x . (See Example 25 for an example of such reasoning.) The second case is that, given x , no such z is reachable. In that case the problem reduces to studying a lower-dimensional model (that is, one with fewer species) for which x may or may not be transient, and potential options for studying this include Theorems 4 or 10 from this paper. //

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Remark 12: The condition that there exists a vector v such that ( vM ) m > 0 for each m ∈ supp( v ) should be thought of as an instability condition on the matrix M . Indeed, if v = 0 then Theorem 10 does not give transience for any states ( x, i ), whereas by Proposition 54 a non-zero v such that ( vM ) m > 0 for each m ∈ supp( v ) exists iff M is Hurwitz-unstable (that is, iff M has an eigenvalue with strictly positive real part). The conclusion of the theorem, then, is that if the mixed matrix M is unstable in a linear algebra sense, then the mixed stochastic process is unstable in the usual Markov chain sense as long as the environment switches rapidly enough. //

texts.orig: The idea of the proof of Theorem 10 will be to define a Lyapunov function using Lemma 6, show that the generator applied to this function is positive as long as the function is sufficiently large, and then apply Theorem 49 to conclude that the claimed states are evanescent. For this final step it will be convenient if the only reactions y → y ′ which are invisible to v in the sense that v · ( y ′ -y ) = 0 are those for which supp( v ) ∩ supp( y ′ -y ) = ∅ . As it turns out, we can assume this without loss of generality, which follows from the next lemma.

texts.orig: Proof. Fix x ∈ Z d ≥ 0 such that supp( v ) ∩ supp( x ) = ∅ . We can view the CRN as a mixed process in one environment with M 1 = M and switching matrix Q = (0). So if we consider the mixed process with matrix κQ , then Theorem 16 says that x is evanescent for the process if κ is small enough and Theorem 10 says that x is evanescent for the process if κ is large enough. But κQ = Q in this case, so either theorem is enough to conclude the claimed result.

texts.orig: In the introduction, we summarized the heuristic of our paper as saying that when the environment switches quickly, the mixed process behaves like the average, whereas when the environment switches slowly the mixed process behaves like the processes in the individual environments. While we believe the heuristic is a good summary of the results of our four main theorems, Theorems 4, 8, 10, and 16, care should be taken about going too far. For instance, one might be tempted to conjecture that when some M i are unstable and others are stable, then the mixed process should be unstable for small enough κ , on the basis that the (Cartesian) product of a stable Markov chain with an unstable Markov chain is unstable. And Example 32 provides some support for this conjecture (think about what happens if the reaction B → 2 B is added to R 1 with rate constant larger than one).

texts.orig: 5 Intermediate Switching
texts.text: 5 Intermediate Switching

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The purpose of this section is to explore possible dynamics for intermediate values of κ . First, we will show that there are models in which the behavior at sufficiently small and sufficiently large values of κ is the same, but at some intermediate κ the behavior changes. Specifically, the model in Example 36 will turn out to be transient for both small and large κ but exponentially ergodic for intermediate κ , whereas the model in Example 40 will be exponentially ergodic for both small and large κ but transient for intermediate κ . Second, building on these examples, in Proposition 42 we will show that there exists models with an arbitrarily large number of phase transitions between exponentially ergodic and transient as the parameter κ is varied, with our preferred behavior at extreme values of κ .
texts.text: The purpose of this section is to explore possible dynamics for intermediate values of κ . First, we will show that there are models in which the behavior at sufficiently small and sufficiently large values of κ is the same, but at some intermediate κ the behavior changes. Specifically, the model in Example 36 will turn out to be transient for both small and large κ but exponentially ergodic for intermediate κ , whereas the model in Example 40 will be exponentially ergodic for both small and large κ but transient for intermediate κ . Second, building on these examples, in Proposition 42 we will show that there exists models with an arbitrarily large number of phase transitions between exponentially ergodic and transient as the parameter κ is varied, with our preferred behavior at extreme values of κ .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recall that Examples 21 and 25 show that there exists models which are transient for small κ and exponentially ergodic for large κ , and vice versa. If these networks are combined into one big network with two non-interacting sets of species, one from the first example and one from the second, the combined network will be transient whenever either model is transient, and exponentially ergodic when both are exponentially ergodic. Thus to construct an example which is transient for κ both small and large but exponentially ergodic for intermediate κ , we just have to combine these two previous examples in such a way that regimes where both are exponentially ergodic overlap. As it turns out, this is straightforward to do by simply adjusting the relative rates of the two models, which is the content of the following example.

texts.orig: Note a fundamental asymmetry in the proof of Proposition 37: The combined (4-species) model is transient when either of the two (2-species) submodels is transient, but is exponentially ergodic only when both of the submodels are exponentially ergodic. As a consequence, it will not be possible to use the same trick to produce a model which is exponentially ergodic for all sufficiently large κ and also all sufficiently small κ , but is nevertheless transient for intermediate κ . We will create such a model via other methods, detailed below. In any case, once we do have such a model, the same trick used in Proposition 37 will allow us to create models with an arbitrarily large number of phase transitions and our choice of behavior (exponentially ergodic or transient) for sufficiently large κ and separately for sufficiently small κ (see Proposition 42).

texts.orig: Remark 39: As mentioned, Proposition 38 is not stated in the maximum possible generality. The proposition involves two groups of two environments, with fast switching within groups and slow switching between groups. But there is no reason other than ease of notation to assume that there are only two groups and/or only two environments within each group, nor even that each group has the same number of environments. Similarly, only convenience leads us to assume that the process divides its time equally between and within the groups. Lastly, note that proposition requires v 1 , v 2 ∈ R d > 0 ; it should be possible to allow for v 1 , v 2 ∈ R d ≥ 0 in the manner of Theorems 10 and 16 by following the proof of either theorem.

However, by construction for each j ∈ { 1 , · · · , N } there exists a κ ∈ ( β j κ j max , β j κ j min ) such that the j -th submodel is evanescent, and thus for such κ the whole model is evanescent. To see that the model has the correct behavior for κ very small, note that all submodels but the first are exponentially ergodic for sufficiently small κ , and hence the whole model will have the same stability behavior for small κ as the first submodel, which recall was chosen to be either exponentially ergodic or evanescent as desired. The verification for large κ is the same.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This section summarizes some definitions and results from linear algebra. No originality is claimed. Our goal is to allow the hypotheses of the main theorems of this paper to be understood in terms that may be more familiar. Specifically, Proposition 51(i) tells us that a hypothesis of Theorem 4 (namely, that there exists a v ∈ R d > 0 with vM ∈ R d < 0 ) is equivalent to the matrix M being Hurwitz stable (having only eigenvalues with negative real part), and similarly for the matrices M i in Theorem 8. Meanwhile, Proposition 54 tells us that the similar conditions in Theorems 10 and 16 are equivalent to the matrices M and M i , respectively, being Hurwitz unstable (having at least one eigenvalue with positive real part).

texts.orig: A matrix M = ( m ij ) is said to be Metzler if m ij ≥ 0 whenever i = j . A matrix M (not necessarily Metzler) is said to be Hurwitz stable if every eigenvalue of M has strictly negative real part; on the other hand, if there exists an eigenvalue of M with strictly positive real part then M is said to be Hurwitz unstable .

texts.orig: Remark 50: Notice that passing to a principle submatrix preserves the Metzler property. In addition, note that because the Frobenius normal form is block-triangular, the eigenvalues of M will be exactly the collective eigenvaules of the Frobenius principal submatrices of M . In particular, M is Hurwitz stable iff every Frobenius principal submatrix of M is Hurwitz stable, and M is Hurwitz unstable iff some Frobenius principal submatrix of M is Hurwitz unstable. //

texts.text: Lemma 53: Suppose that M is a d × d Metzler matrix. Let r denote the largest eigenvalue of M . Let r ′ denote the maximum over ∅ = I ⊊ [ d ] of the largest eigenvalue of the (Metzler) matrix M I . Then r ′ ≤ r .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. This lemma, but for specifically non-negative matrices rather than general Metzler matrices, is Proposition 4 in [14, Chapter XIII, Section 3]. To extend to Metzler matrices M , let N := M + αI d for some α ∈ R > 0 no smaller in absolute value than all diagonal entries of M , so that N is non-negative. Then the largest eigenvalue of N is r + α . Moreover, for each ∅ = I ⊆ [ d ] the largest eigenvalue of N I is α plus the largest eigenvalue of M I . It follows that the maximum over I of the largest eigenvalue of N I is r ′ + α . Since the lemma holds for non-negative matrices we have r ′ + α ≤ r + α ; the lemma in full generality follows.

texts.orig: (ii) = ⇒ (iii): Suppose that I is nonempty and M I has an increasing direction. Then by Proposition 51(ii), all Frobenius principal submatrices of M I are Hurwitz unstable. It follows that M I is Hurwitz unstable (see, e.g., Remark 50).
texts.text: (ii) = ⇒ (iii): Suppose that I is nonempty and M I has an increasing direction. Then by Proposition 51(ii), all Frobenius principal submatrices of M I are Hurwitz unstable. It follows that M I is Hurwitz unstable (see, e.g., Remark 50).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (iii) = ⇒ (ii): Suppose that J is nonempty and M J is Hurwitz unstable; that is, M J has an eigenvalue with strictly positive real part. Consider the Frobenius normal form of M J . As discussed in Remark 50, the eigenvalues of M J are exactly the eigenvalues of the Frobenius principal submatrices of M J , so there must exist a nonempty I ⊆ J such that the Frobenius principal submatrix ( M J ) I = M I has an eigenvalue with strictly positive real part. But then M I is an irreducible Hurwitz-unstable matrix, so by Proposition 51(ii) we conclude that M I has an increasing direction.

texts.orig: (iii) = ⇒ (iv): Following the notation of Lemma 53, let r denote the largest eigenvalue of M and let r ′ denote the maximal largest eigenvalue of a proper submatrix of M . Suppose there exists a Hurwitz unstable submatrix of M . Either this submatrix is all of M and we are done, or the submatrix is included in the maximum defining r ′ and so 0 < r ′ . But by Lemma 53 we know r ′ ≤ r , so 0 < r and we are done.
